min_doctor_qualification: 1 Year training in maternal fetal medicine/PDCC/Equivalent certificate course
clinical_indications: Diagnose and treat fetal severe anemia
Diagnose and evaluate therapeutic response in NAIT

preauth.label: Detailed Clinical notes with history, indications, symptoms, signs, examination findings and advice for admission

claim.label: Nuchal translucency (NT) and Early TIFFA (Targeted imaging for fetal anomalies) scan reports

ppd_preauth.q: Are all vitals, detailed history, symptoms, signs, physical examination including local examination, indication for procedure, planned line of treatment and advice for admission detailed in the clinical notes?
Was the prenatal screen of the women positive indicating need for fetal genetic analysis?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and line of treatment available?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Did the NT/Early TIFFA scan reports submitted show any abnormality and indicative of performing the procedure?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Prenatal screen of the women not documented as positive or not indicating need for fetal genetic analysis
Nuchal translucency (NT) and Early TIFFA scan reports not submitted, or not showing abnormality indicative of performing the procedure

Detailed Clinical notes missing essential information such as history, indications, symptoms, signs, examination findings, planned line of treatment, or advice for admission at pre-authorization stage
Detailed indoor case papers missing daily vitals or line of treatment at claim submission
Detailed procedure/operative notes not available at claim submission
Discharge summary not available or lacking follow-up advice at claim submission

additional_information.special_empanelment: Care at Tertiary hospital, procedure done under ultrasound guidance. Facility should be registered as per PCPNDT law for intervention procedures.
clinical_key_pointers: Ultrasound-guided fetal blood sampling (FBS), also known as cordocentesis, or percutaneous umbilical cord blood sampling is performed under local anesthetic usually from 18 weeks of gestation.
Ultrasound-guided FBS is the only procedure that provides direct access to the fetal circulation.
Umbilical vein is preferred. The advantages are: (a) vein is larger in size (b) causes less bradycardia and (c) less hemorrhage.
Fetal aneuploidy for karyotyping (Rarely used in current practice; largely replaced by CVS or amniocentesis with FISH, or by NIPT)
Determine fetal blood type and platelet antigen status (Largely replaced by other tests, eg, NIPT, CVS, or amniocentesis, and molecular testing)
Diagnose genetic disorders (eg, hemophilia, thalassemia) (Largely replaced by CVS or amniocentesis for molecular genetic diagnosis)

Measurement of biochemical or other serum markers for fetal disease (eg, fetal infection, thyroid function) (Largely replaced by amniocentesis and PCR (eg, infection); rarely needed (eg, thyroid function))
Direct intravascular therapy (Reported rarely, most commonly for failed maternal systemic treatment of fetal supraventricular tachycardia)
risks_complications: This invasive procedure may lead to abortion, preterm labor and intrauterine fetal death.
These may be due to bleeding, cord hematoma formation, infection (amnionitis), feto-maternal hemorrhage or preterm rupture of membranes.
